narrative: I'm sorry to share that our admissions model predicted a 49% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—it's a close call, but just over the threshold we use to ensure strong outcomes for our students. You have a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which is above the typical 3.21 average, along with an upper-middle family income bracket, female gender, white racial background, and plans to attend full-time, all of which align closely with or exceed many applicants' profiles. However, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1 and Year 3 ranking of 2 are notably lower than the averages of about 5.7 and 5.5, respectively, and your LSAT score of 31 falls short of the usual 36.7. The model's decision was influenced most strongly by your Year 3 decile ranking, which had the biggest push toward predicting bar failure compared to what the model expects from successful students. Next in influence was your family income bracket, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, both nudging the prediction toward failure relative to typical high performers. Your LSAT score ranked fourth in impact, also contributing toward the failure prediction given how it stacks up against stronger scorers. Even your above-average undergraduate GPA played a role in the same direction, though less prominently, along with your race, gender, and full-time status trailing further behind in influence. Overall, these factors together tipped the scales just enough for the concerning prediction, especially since your early and later school rankings and LSAT are below what the model sees in bar passers. To improve your chances next time, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost that score toward the average or higher, as it's a key changeable factor that directly sways bar success predictions. Strengthening your academic decile performance in future studies could also help demonstrate growth beyond your Year 1 and 3 results. With your strong GPA as a foundation, targeted prep for the bar itself might address the model's concerns about first-try passing. We encourage you to reapply after addressing these areas—you're close, and small improvements could flip the outcome. Keep pushing; many students turn predictions like this around with focused effort.
